items.title: Serena Williams in doubt for Wimbledon doubles with sister Venus
items.standfirst: Former world No.1 Williams lost her celebrated singles return at Wimbledon to Australia’s Maya Joint, but an injury could stop her playing doubles.
items.content: Former world No.1 Williams lost her celebrated singles return at Wimbledon to Australia’s Maya Joint, but an injury could stop her playing doubles.

items.title: Who is Maya Joint, Australian conqueror of Serena at Wimbledon?
items.standfirst: Maya Joint was born in April 2006 when Serena Williams had already won seven Grand Slam singles titles. So who is the 20-year-old who just defeated a tennis legend in her return to the All England Club?
items.content: Maya Joint was born in April 2006 when Serena Williams had already won seven Grand Slam singles titles. So who is the 20-year-old who just defeated a tennis legend in her return to the All England Club?

items.title: Why Maya Joint had to catch a glimpse of Serena Williams before their match
items.standfirst: Australian Maya Joint had to catch a glimpse of Serena Williams in the days leading up to their Wimbledon opener just to remove some of the mythos around the 23-time grand slam champion.
items.content: Australian Maya Joint had to catch a glimpse of Serena Williams in the days leading up to their Wimbledon opener just to remove some of the mythos around the 23-time grand slam champion.

items.title: Australian rising star takes down Serena Williams in Wimbledon return

items.title: Authorities renew safety warning as Neil the seal destroys barrier
items.standfirst: Neil the seal has become one of Tasmania’s biggest celebrities, with social media videos showing him knocking over bollards and leaning on cars. But wildlife experts are concerned his popularity could put him and the public at risk. Some large animals have been euthanised overseas due to public safety concerns, but Tasmanian authorities say that option is not part of their management approach.
items.content: Neil the seal has become one of Tasmania’s biggest celebrities, with social media videos showing him knocking over bollards and leaning on cars. But wildlife experts are concerned his popularity could put him and the public at risk. Some large animals have been euthanised overseas due to public safety concerns, but Tasmanian authorities say that option is not part of their management approach.
